32	# DAG Walker
33	
34	You take a **seed declaration** and walk UP its dependency cone in the leandag graph, making the cone **complete**: every declaration the seed transitively depends on must have (1) a real statement block, (2) an informal proof so its effort is finite (not ∞), and (3) a `\uses{}` set that matches the dependencies its mathematics actually uses. You turn ∞-effort cones into finite ones and you make the frontier trustworthy — a node is only honestly "ready to prove" when its `\uses{}` is complete and every dependency is done or itself on the way.
35	
36	You are the dependency-completeness agent. Unlike a blueprint-writer (one chapter, precise directive), you follow the graph wherever it goes — across chapters — filling whatever is missing beneath the seed.
37	
38	## Directive Format
39	
40	```markdown
41	# DAG Walker Directive
42	
43	## Slug
44	<slug>
45	
46	## Seed
47	<the blueprint label to walk up from, e.g. thm:pic_zero_dimension_equals_genus.
48	 May be an ∞-source, the project goal, or a frontier node to make honest.>
49	
50	## Strategy context
51	<the slice of STRATEGY.md the dispatcher extracted — what this cone supports in
52	 the overall arc. You do NOT read STRATEGY.md yourself.>
53	
54	## Depth / scope
55	<how far up to walk: "the whole cone to the axioms" (default), or "one level —
56	 the seed's direct \uses only", or "stop at chapter X". Plus any out-of-scope
57	 declarations.>
58	
59	## References
60	- <path/to/reference.md or pointer>: <which results live here>
61	```
62	
63	## How you walk
64	
65	Use **`archon dag-query`** (read-only) to navigate — do not eyeball the graph or trust memory:
66	
67	```
68	archon dag-query node      --node <seed> --json      # inspect the seed
69	archon dag-query ancestors --node <seed> --json      # the full dependency closure (everything it transitively uses)
70	archon dag-query gaps --json                         # all ∞ holes, project-wide
71	```
72	
73	`archon` is on PATH. JSON goes to stdout (the banner to stderr), so `--json` is parseable. For each node in the cone, check the **three completeness conditions** below, fix what's broken, and **recurse**: a block you add has its own dependencies — add those too, walking up until you bottom out at a done node (`\leanok` / `\mathlibok`) or an axiom (a node with no further dependencies).
74	
75	## The three completeness conditions (per node in the cone)
76	
77	1. **Exists with a statement.** Every `\uses{label}` in the cone must resolve to a real blueprint block. A `\uses{}` pointing at a label that exists in no chapter is a **broken edge** (`archon dag-query` / `leandag build --json` reports these). Fix it: correct the label, or — if the dependency is real but unblueprinted — **add the missing `\begin{lemma/definition}` block** (statement + `\label` + `\lean` + `\uses`) in the appropriate chapter.
78	
79	2. **Has an informal proof — finite effort, not ∞.** A node whose `effort_local` is ∞ is a statement with **no `\begin{proof}`** (and no sorry-free Lean): a roadmap hole. Formalizing it would be blind progress. Give it a proof sketch:
80	   - If the result is genuinely the project's to prove, **write the informal proof** (mathematical prose, project notation, `\uses{}` for each step's dependencies), grounded in a reference per citation discipline below.
81	   - If **Mathlib already provides it**, make it a **Mathlib dependency anchor** (`\mathlibok`, see below) instead of proving it.
82	   - If it is genuinely hard / not-yet-in-the-literature / needs novel mathematics, **do not fabricate a proof** — leave it ∞, record it under "Could not complete", and surface any strategy implication.
83	
84	3. **`\uses{}` is complete — the trust anchor.** Read the node's statement and informal proof, and verify that **every mathematical fact it actually relies on is declared in `\uses{}`**. If the proof of `T` invokes lemma `L` but `T` does not `\uses{lem:L}`, add it — and if `lem:L` has no blueprint block, create it (condition 1) and walk up into it. This is the single most important check: an under-declared `\uses{}` makes a node *look* ready (its missing dependency is invisible to the frontier) when its real foundation is not done. Do not over-declare either: a `\uses{}` to something the proof does not use is noise — remove it only when you are sure it is spurious.
85	
86	A node is **complete** when it is `\leanok` / `\mathlibok`, or it has a real statement + a finite-effort proof + a complete `\uses{}` whose targets are themselves complete or on the way. Stop walking up through `\mathlibok` anchors — they are the leaves of the proof-obligation tree.
87	
88	## Markers (same rules as every blueprint agent)
89	
90	- **Never add `\leanok`.** It is earned by a sorry-free Lean proof and set by the deterministic `sync_leanok` phase. Not yours.
91	- **`\mathlibok` only on a genuine Mathlib dependency anchor.** When the cone bottoms out at a result Mathlib already provides, write the statement, point `\lean{}` at the **real Mathlib declaration**, mark `\mathlibok`, and add a `\textit{Provided by Mathlib.}` line — no `\begin{proof}` needed. This gives leandag a done node so the `\uses{}` resolves and the effort is finite. **Anti-hallucination rule:** mark `\mathlibok` ONLY when the statement genuinely exists in Mathlib in the form you wrote. A wrong `\mathlibok` is worse than an ∞ hole — the loop skips proving a real gap. If unsure it is in Mathlib, leave it ∞ and report it.
92	
93	## Citation discipline (the hard rule)
94	
95	Every block you write that derives from external reference material needs: a `% SOURCE:` comment (`<pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`), a `% SOURCE QUOTE:` with the **verbatim** original-language statement copied from that local file, a `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` before the proof environment when applicable, and a visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}` first line. **Never write any of these from training memory.** If you lack the local source, spawn a `reference-retriever` (Bash, blocking — needs `references/**` in your write-domain), wait for it, open the new `references/<slug>.md`, then write the citation block. If retrieval fails, leave the block flagged and report it INCOMPLETE — never substitute a paraphrase or a remembered quote.
96	
97	## What you must / must not do
98	
99	- **Stay in your write-domain** (`blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`). The CLI rejects writes outside it. You do NOT edit `.lean` files, `content.tex`, `macros/`, or any state file. A needed macro is a "Notes" item.
100	- **Keep every chapter valid LaTeX** (matched `\begin`/`\end`, balanced braces in `\label`/`\uses`/`\lean`).
101	- **Mathematical prose, not Lean syntax.** No tactics, no typeclass notes, no project-history narrative — the blueprint reads as a standalone document.
102	- **Re-query after editing.** Run `archon dag-query ancestors --node <seed>` again at the end: the cone should now have zero broken `\uses{}` and (except for declared "Could not complete" nodes) zero ∞ effort. Fix and re-check until it converges or only genuine-gap nodes remain.
103	
104	## Workflow
105	
106	1. Read the directive. Inspect the seed: `archon dag-query node --node <seed> --json`.
107	2. Pull the cone: `archon dag-query ancestors --node <seed> --json`. Note which nodes are ∞ (need a proof), which `\uses{}` are broken, and which blocks may be missing.
108	3. Read the relevant chapters on disk and `references/summary.md`; read every reference your directive names.
109	4. Walk the cone bottom-up (axioms/Mathlib first), applying the three checks. Add missing blocks, add missing `\uses{}` edges, write missing proofs or Mathlib anchors. Recurse into anything new you introduce.
110	5. For any block needing a source you don't have locally, spawn a `reference-retriever`, wait, read the file, then write the cited block.
111	6. Re-query the cone; fix residual broken edges / ∞ holes until only genuine gaps remain.
112	7. Write your report.
113	
114	## Logging
115	
116	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/dag-walker-<slug>.md` (or the nested `task_results/<parent-slug>/...` path your invocation names).

137	- Proof sketch for `<label>` — <brief shape; ∞ → finite>
138	- Mathlib anchor `<label>` → `\lean{<real Mathlib decl>}` `\mathlibok`
139	
140	## \uses edges added/fixed (the completeness fixes)
141	- `<node>` now `\uses{<dep>}` — its proof invokes <dep> but didn't declare it (made the frontier honest).
142	- Fixed broken `\uses{<bad>}` in `<node>` → `<good>` / created `<dep>`.
143

145	- `<label>` — no informal proof exists (hard / novel / not in literature). Left ∞. <strategy implication, if any>
146

98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	Archon talks back to the user through **`TO_USER.md`** — a single, *persistent* notice board surfaced as a UI banner. It is **shared and writable by every agent** (plan, prover, review). It is no longer cleared at the start of each review phase; content survives across iters until an agent prunes it. The discipline every writer follows:
112	
113	- **Concise**: the whole file stays ≤ 2–3 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.
114	- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.
115	- **Notice board, never a question queue**: the loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters. Never write "awaiting your decision", an options menu, a "where to reply", or "default to X if no reply" — anything implying the loop is paused for a human. State decisions as *made* and blockers as *worked-around-as-far-as-possible*; the user steers asynchronously via `USER_HINTS.md`.
116	
117	## Agent Roles
118	
119	### Plan Agent
120	
121	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
122	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
123	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
124	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
125	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
126	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
127	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
128	
129	### Prover Agent
130	
131	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
132	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
133	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
134	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
135	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
136	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
137	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
138	
139	### Subagents
140	
141	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
142	
143	### Review Agent
144	
145	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
146	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
147	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
148	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
149	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
150	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
151	
152	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
153	
154	These run automatically between phases:
155	
156	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
157	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
158	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.
159

2	\label{chap:Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension}

32	\section{Setup and statement}
33	\label{sec:thm32_setup}
34	
35	Fix an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\), a smooth variety \(X\) over \(\bar k\) (in the
36	project's convention: a geometrically integral, geometrically reduced, separated scheme of
37	finite type over \(\bar k\) that is moreover smooth, hence nonsingular at every closed point),
38	and an abelian variety \(A\) over \(\bar k\) (in particular \(A\) is complete, smooth, and a
39	group object in \(\mathrm{Sch}/\bar k\)). A rational map
40	\(f \colon X \dashrightarrow A\) is, in the project's notation, the equivalence class of a
41	regular morphism \(\varphi_U \colon U \to A\) defined on a dense open subset
42	\(U \subseteq X\), two such pairs \((U, \varphi_U)\) and \((U', \varphi_{U'})\) being equivalent
43	if \(\varphi_U\) and \(\varphi_{U'}\) agree on \(U \cap U'\).
44	
45	The result of this chapter is:
46	
47	\begin{theorem}
48	
49	\leanok
50	[Milne Theorem~3.2: a rational map from a nonsingular variety to an abelian variety extends]
51	  \label{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends}
52	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.extend_to_av}
53	  \uses{thm:codim_one_extension, lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy}
54	
55	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Theorem~3.2, \S I.3, doc p.~17
56	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 23).
57	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
58	  % "THEOREM 3.2. A rational map \(\alpha\colon V--\to A\) from a nonsingular
59	  %  variety to an abelian variety is defined on the whole of \(V\)."
60	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Theorem~3.2, \S I.3, p.~17.}
61	
62	  Let \(X\) be a nonsingular variety over an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\), let \(A\) be
63	  an abelian variety over \(\bar k\), and let \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow A\) be a rational
64	  map. Then \(f\) is defined on the whole of \(X\): there is a unique regular morphism
65	  \(\widetilde f \colon X \to A\) whose restriction to some (equivalently, every) dense open
66	  set of definition \(U \subseteq X\) of \(f\) agrees with \(\varphi_U\).
67	\end{theorem}
68	
69	The uniqueness clause is immediate from \(X\) being reduced and separated: two regular
70	morphisms \(X \to A\) that agree on a dense open subset agree everywhere. The substance is
71	existence of the extension. Milne deduces it in one line by combining the codim-\(\geq 2\)
72	extension result for rational maps to a complete variety with the codim-1 indeterminacy
73	structure result for rational maps to a group variety:
74	
75	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
76	% "PROOF. Combine Theorem 3.1 with the next lemma."
77	\begin{proof}
78	  
79	  Milne's one-line proof is: \emph{combine Theorem~3.1 with Lemma~3.3}.
80	  Theorem~3.1 (the codim-\(\geq 2\) extension theorem for a rational map from a normal
81	  variety to a complete variety; valuative criterion of properness) is
82	  \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} of the present project; Lemma~3.3 (the
83	  pure-codimension-\(1\) structure of the indeterminacy locus of a rational map into a
84	  group variety) is \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} of the same chapter.
85	
86	  Concretely: let \(U \subseteq X\) be a maximal open set of definition of \(f\), and let
87	  \(Z := X \setminus U\) be its complement.
88	  \begin{enumerate}
89	    \item By \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} (Milne~3.1, the valuative-criterion extension
90	      to codim \(\geq 2\)), applied with the normal variety \(X\) and the complete variety
91	      \(A\), we have \(\mathrm{codim}_X(Z) \geq 2\).
92	    \item By \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} (Milne~3.3, the pure-codim-\(1\)
93	      indeterminacy structure for maps into the group variety \(A\)), the closed subset
94	      \(Z\) is either empty or of pure codimension \(1\) in \(X\).
95	    \item Both conditions together force \(Z = \emptyset\): a non-empty pure-codimension-\(1\)
96	      closed subset has codimension exactly \(1\), contradicting \(\mathrm{codim}_X(Z) \geq 2\).
97	  \end{enumerate}
98	  Therefore \(U = X\) and \(f\) admits a regular representative on all of \(X\), which is the
99	  required regular morphism \(\widetilde f \colon X \to A\). Uniqueness is the standard
100	  reduced-and-separated agreement principle (see the paragraph preceding the theorem).
101	\end{proof}
102	
103	\begin{remark}
104	  \label{rmk:thm32_role_of_ab}
105	  \textbf{Where Auslander--Buchsbaum enters.} The combination above relies on
106	  \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}, which is itself proved in
107	  \cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension} in two stages: a codim-\(1\) step
108	  (valuative criterion of properness, applied at the DVR
109	  \(\mathcal O_{X, Z}\) of each codimension-\(1\) point \(Z\) --- works because \(X\) is normal
110	  and \(A\) is complete) and a codim-\(\geq 2\) step (depth-\(\geq 2\) at codim-\(\geq 2\) points,
111	  giving the local-cohomology / Hartshorne~III.8 sheaf-extension property). The
112	  depth-\(\geq 2\) input on a smooth (regular) variety is exactly what
113	  \cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay} of \cref{chap:Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum} supplies:
114	  for a regular local ring \((\mathcal O_{X, x}, \mathfrak m_x)\) of Krull dimension \(d\), the
115	  Auslander--Buchsbaum formula \cref{thm:auslander_buchsbaum} (applied to \(M = R\)
116	  trivially, or to the depth-via-regular-sequence characterisation directly) gives
117	  \(\mathrm{depth}(\mathcal O_{X, x}) = d\), so in particular
118	  \(\mathrm{depth}(\mathcal O_{X, x}) \geq 2\) whenever \(\mathrm{codim}_X(\{x\}) \geq 2\).
119	  Concretely, when \(X\) is a smooth surface (e.g.\ \(\mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb P^1\) or
120	  \(C \times C\) for a smooth projective curve \(C\)), every closed point \(x\) is regular of
121	  dimension \(2\), so \(\mathcal O_{X, x}\) is Cohen--Macaulay of depth \(2\); sections of
122	  \(\mathcal O_X\) on \(U = X \setminus \{x\}\) extend uniquely across \(x\), and the same is
123	  true for affine-locally pulled-back sections of \(\mathcal O_A\) along \(f\).
124	
125	  Auslander--Buchsbaum therefore does not appear by name in the proof above; it appears
126	  inside \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}'s codim-\(\geq 2\) half. The
127	  \cref{thm:auslander_buchsbaum} dependency is recorded on
128	  \cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} to keep the leanblueprint dependency graph
129	  faithful: A.4.c truly does sit on top of A.4.b through A.4.a.
130	\end{remark}
131	
132	\section{Why the proof is char-free}
133	\label{sec:thm32_char_free}
134	
135	Each of the two inputs is char-free:
136	\begin{itemize}
137	  \item \emph{Theorem~3.1 / codim-\(\geq 2\) extension} (\cref{thm:codim_one_extension}):
138	    the proof uses only the valuative criterion of properness and depth-\(\geq 2\) extension
139	    of sections on regular local rings; both are characteristic-agnostic.
140	  \item \emph{Lemma~3.3 / pure-codim-\(1\) indeterminacy for maps into a group variety}
141	    (\cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy}): Milne's proof forms the difference map
142	    \(\Phi(x, y) := \varphi(x)\,\varphi(y)^{-1} \colon X \times X \dashrightarrow A\) and
143	    examines its zero/pole divisor along the diagonal --- a Weil-divisor argument that
144	    works for any algebraically closed base field. The argument uses only the group law
145	    on \(A\) (its smoothness as a group object), the regularity of \(X\) (so divisors and
146	    DVRs at codim-\(1\) points are available), and the existence of a divisor of zeros and
147	    poles for a nonzero rational function on a nonsingular variety. None of these inputs
148	    has a characteristic restriction.
149	\end{itemize}
150	Consequently \cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} holds over every algebraically closed
151	field, in arbitrary characteristic. (Milne states this proof verbatim in characteristic-free
152	generality at \S I.3, pp.~16--17.)
153	
154	\section{Application: the symmetric-product Albanese map (gating A.4.d)}
155	\label{sec:thm32_application_to_a4d}
156	
157	The downstream consumer is A.4.d (\cref{chap:Albanese_AlbaneseUP}):
158	the Albanese universal property of the Jacobian \(J = \Pic^0_{C/k}\). Milne's proof of
159	Proposition~6.1 of \S III.6 (\emph{Abelian Varieties}, p.~104) constructs the unique
160	morphism \(\psi \colon J \to A\) factoring a given pointed regular morphism
161	\(\varphi \colon C \to A\) through the canonical \(f_P \colon C \to J\) as follows. The
162	symmetric power \(C^{(g)}\) admits a regular morphism \(\varphi^{(g)} \colon C^{(g)} \to A\)
163	defined by
164	\[
165	  \varphi^{(g)}\bigl(P_1 + \dots + P_g\bigr) \;=\; \varphi(P_1) + \dots + \varphi(P_g),
166	\]
167	and a birational morphism \(C^{(g)} \dashrightarrow J\) (the Abel--Jacobi map). One then
168	seeks a morphism \(J \to A\) making the obvious diagram commute. The construction goes via
169	\(C^{(g)} \dashrightarrow A\) followed by descent to \(J\); the rational map
170	\(C^{(g)} \dashrightarrow A\) obtained at the intermediate step is exactly the kind that
171	\cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} promotes to a regular morphism, because \(C^{(g)}\) is
172	a nonsingular variety (Milne~III.3) and \(A\) is an abelian variety. Without
173	\cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} this step has no replacement: Milne's autoduality
174	shortcut (\(J \cong J^\vee\)) routes through the theorem of the cube, which the project
175	excised at iter-163; see the analysis in \cref{sec:Jacobian_routeA4_albaneseUP} of
176	\cref{chap:Jacobian}.
177	
178	\section{Lean encoding}
179	\label{sec:thm32_lean_encoding}
180	
181	The Lean target file is the new
182	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/Thm32RationalMapExtension.lean}. The single declaration
183	this chapter targets is the theorem
184	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.extend\_to\_av}, with signature (in informal
185	notation):
186	\[
187	  \texttt{X : Scheme} \;\to\; \texttt{[Smooth X over \(\bar k\)]} \;\to\;
188	  \texttt{A : AbelianVariety \(\bar k\)} \;\to\;
189	  (f : \texttt{X.RationalMap A}) \;\to\;
190	  \exists ! \, \widetilde f \colon X \to A, \;\; \widetilde f \;\text{extends}\; f.
191	\]
192	The proof body imports \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}'s exports and
193	\cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay}'s exports from
194	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/CodimOneExtension.lean} and
195	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/AuslanderBuchsbaum.lean} respectively, and combines them
196	in the three-step pattern above (codim \(\geq 2\) from A.4.a + pure-codim \(1\) from A.4.a
197	\(\Rightarrow\) empty indeterminacy locus).
198	
199	The Mathlib namespace target is
200	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap} (already housed in
201	\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Birational.RationalMap} at the project's pinned commit;
202	see \cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}, where the same namespace hosts the
203	order-at-a-prime-divisor map). The extension is therefore a method on the existing
204	\texttt{Scheme.RationalMap} structure rather than a free-standing predicate, allowing
205	downstream consumers in \cref{chap:Albanese_AlbaneseUP} to call it as
206	\texttt{f.extend\_to\_av}.
207	
208	No new core axiom is required; the entire chapter is a two-line combination of A.4.a and
209	A.4.b. The chapter is therefore inexpensive on the prover side (the LOC budget is dominated
210	by A.4.a and A.4.b themselves); its purpose is to record the precise statement and the
211	exact dependency wiring so that A.4.d can consume a single named lemma.
212	
213	\section{Out of scope}
214	\label{sec:thm32_out_of_scope}
215	
216	This chapter packages \emph{only} the theorem
217	\cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} (Milne~3.2) and the immediate proof combining its
218	two inputs. The following adjacent content is explicitly out of scope here:
219	\begin{itemize}
220	  \item \textbf{The codim-\(\geq 2\) extension theorem (Milne Theorem~3.1) itself.}
221	    Proved in \cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension} as
222	    \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}; this chapter only consumes it.
223	  \item \textbf{The pure-codim-\(1\) indeterminacy lemma (Milne Lemma~3.3) itself.}
224	    Proved in \cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension} as
225	    \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy}; this chapter only consumes it.
226	  \item \textbf{The Auslander--Buchsbaum formula and its Cohen--Macaulay corollary
227	    (Milne~\(\equiv\) Stacks 090V / 00OD).} Proved in
228	    \cref{chap:Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum} as \cref{thm:auslander_buchsbaum} and
229	    \cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay}; this chapter only consumes them (indirectly,
230	    inside \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}; see \cref{rmk:thm32_role_of_ab}).
231	  \item \textbf{Milne Theorem~3.4 / Corollary~3.7 / Theorem~3.8 / Proposition~3.9 /
232	    Proposition~3.10.} The remainder of Milne~\S I.3 (the special cases ``rational map
233	    from a product of nonsingular varieties to an AV vanishing on the axes is constant'',
234	    ``rational map between AVs = homomorphism + translation'', ``birationally equivalent
235	    AVs are isomorphic'', ``every \(\mathbb A^1 \dashrightarrow A\) is constant'',
236	    ``every rational map from a unirational variety to an AV is constant'') is downstream
237	    of \cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} but is not needed by A.4 in the project's
238	    current routing: the genus-\(0\) base case
239	    \cref{rmk:base_case_fourth_route} closes
240	    via the proven Rigidity Lemma + \(\mathbb G_m\)-scaling shortcut without invoking any of
241	    these. They are recorded as off-path in
242	    \cref{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity}.
243	  \item \textbf{The Albanese universal property of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) (Milne Proposition~6.1).}
244	    Consumed by A.4.d, proved in \cref{chap:Albanese_AlbaneseUP}; this chapter only
245	    provides the technical extension lemma that A.4.d invokes when promoting
246	    \(C^{(g)} \dashrightarrow A\) to \(C^{(g)} \to A\).
247	  \item \textbf{Generalisations beyond an algebraically closed base field.} Milne's
248	    Theorem~3.2 is stated for varieties over an algebraically closed field; the
249	    project's positive-genus arm runs over \(\bar k\) throughout (\cref{chap:RigidityKbar}).
250	    Descent of the extension to a non-algebraically-closed base, if ever needed, would
251	    require additional Galois-descent machinery and is not in scope.
252	\end{itemize}

1509	  Let \(X\) be a nonsingular variety over \(\bar k\), let \(Y\) be a variety over \(\bar k\),
1510	  let \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) be a rational map, and let \(W \subseteq X\) be a
1511	  prime divisor with generic point \(\eta_W \in W\) (so
1512	  \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{\eta_W\}} = 1\)). Let \((U, \varphi_U)\) be any representative of
1513	  \(f\) with \(U \cap (X \smallsetminus W) \neq \emptyset\). The following are equivalent:
1514	  \begin{enumerate}
1515	    \item \(f\) is defined at \(\eta_W\) (i.e.\ \(\eta_W \in \mathrm{Dom}(f)\), equivalently
1516	      \(W \not\subseteq Z(f)\)).
1517	    \item For some (equivalently, every) affine open neighbourhood \(V \subseteq Y\) of
1518	      the generic image of \(f\) near \(W\), every regular function $g \in
1519	      \mathcal O_Y(V)\( pulls back to a rational function \)\varphi_U^\ast(g) \in K(X)$
1520	      satisfying
1521	      \[
1522	        \ord_W\bigl(\varphi_U^\ast(g)\bigr) \;\geq\; 0.
1523	      \]
1524	  \end{enumerate}
1525	
1526	  Consequently: \(f\) is codim-\(1\)-indeterminacy-free
1527	  (\cref{def:codim_one_indeterminacy}) if and only if, for every prime divisor
1528	  \(W \subseteq X\) and every affine open \(V \subseteq Y\) as above, the pullback
1529	  \(\varphi_U^\ast(g)\) has \(\ord_W \geq 0\) for all \(g \in \mathcal O_Y(V)\).
1530	\end{theorem}

1532	\begin{proof}
1533	  
1534	  By \cref{lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr}, the local ring \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta_W}\) is a
1535	  DVR with fraction field \(K(X)\) and uniformiser determined by \(W\); by
1536	  \cref{def:order_at_point} its valuation is exactly \(\ord_W\). The local ring at
1537	  \(\eta_W\) is therefore characterised inside \(K(X)\) as the subring
1538	  \[
1539	    \mathcal O_{X, \eta_W} \;=\; \bigl\{\, h \in K(X) \;\big|\; \ord_W(h) \geq 0
1540	    \,\bigr\} \cup \{0\}.
1541	  \]
1542	  The rational map \(f\) is defined at \(\eta_W\) if and only if, for any affine open
1543	  \(V = \operatorname{Spec} A \subseteq Y\) around the image \(\overline{f(\eta_W)}\),
1544	  the pullback \(\varphi_U^\ast \colon A \to K(X)\) lands in
1545	  \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta_W}\): this is the standard local characterisation of where a
1546	  rational map is regular at a codim-\(1\) point of a smooth variety (it is the
1547	  ``regular = no pole'' criterion). By the displayed identity, the latter is
1548	  equivalent to \(\ord_W(\varphi_U^\ast(g)) \geq 0\) for every \(g \in A\), which is
1549	  condition (2). The equivalence (1)~\(\Leftrightarrow\)~(2) is therefore established.
1550	
1551	  For the consequence, \(f\) is codim-\(1\)-indeterminacy-free iff no prime divisor \(W\) is
1552	  contained in \(Z(f)\), which by the equivalence above is iff the \(\ord_W \geq 0\)
1553	  condition holds for every prime divisor \(W\).
1554	\end{proof}

1213	
1214	  \emph{Step 1: rule out codim-\(1\) components.} Suppose for contradiction that \(Z(f)\)
1215	  contains a prime divisor \(W \subseteq X\) (i.e.\ a codim-\(1\) irreducible component).
1216	  Let \(\eta \in W\) be its generic point, so \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{\eta\}} = 1\). By
1217	  \cref{lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr}, the local ring \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta}\) is a discrete
1218	  valuation ring with fraction field the function field \(K(X)\). Any representative
1219	  \((U, \varphi_U) \in f\) has \(U \cap \mathrm{Dom}(f) \neq \emptyset\) and so determines
1220	  a \(\bar k\)-algebra map from the function field \(K(Y)\) of \(Y\) to \(K(X)\): explicitly,
1221	  \(\varphi_U\) defines a morphism of schemes \(\operatorname{Spec}(K(X)) \to Y\) (since
1222	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(K(X))\) is the generic point of \(X\)). By the valuative criterion
1223	  of properness applied to the complete variety \(Y\)
1224	  (\emph{cf.}\ Hartshorne~II.4.7, cited verbatim in Milne~3.1's proof), this morphism
1225	  extends to a morphism \(\operatorname{Spec}(\mathcal O_{X, \eta}) \to Y\). Standard
1226	  algebraic-geometry spreading then exhibits an open neighbourhood \(V\) of \(\eta\) in
1227	  \(X\) on which \(f\) has a regular representative; in particular $\eta \in
1228	  \mathrm{Dom}(f)\(, contradicting \)\eta \in W \subseteq Z(f)\(. Hence no codim-\)1$
1229	  component of \(X\) lies in \(Z(f)\), i.e.\ \(Z(f)\) has codimension \(\geq 2\).

1240	  (any small affine neighbourhood \(V \ni x\)) has \(H^0_{\{x\}}(V, \mathcal O_X) = 0\)
1241	  and \(H^1_{\{x\}}(V, \mathcal O_X) = 0\) (the depth-\(\geq 2\) characterisation of

Earlier framings in this chapter (the piece-(iv) ``DEFERRED, not on the C.2.d critical path'' disposition and the ``No-Serre-duality layer'') asserted that the chart-algebra route \emph{avoids} Serre duality; \textbf{that is false}, and is corrected throughout \cref{sec:RigidityKbar_shared_pile}: \(H^0(C, \Omega_{C/\bar k}) = 0\) is \emph{on} the critical path for producing \(\mathrm df = 0\), not avoided.
2:40:\paragraph{Iter-152 alg-closed pivot.} The project committed an architectural pivot: rigidity is proved \textbf{only over an algebraically closed base field \(\bar k\) of characteristic zero}, and the constancy conclusion descends to a general base field \(k\) downstream. The reason is that the chart-algebra core lemma \cref{lem:KaehlerDifferential_mem_range_algebraMap_of_D_eq_zero} (KDM) is mathematically \emph{false} as a bare \(B\)-only algebra lemma: a prover lane exhibited two counterexamples that satisfy every hypothesis yet have \(\ker D \supsetneq \operatorname{range}(\mathtt{algebraMap})\) --- namely \(B = k \times k\) (finite \'etale over \(k\)) and \(B = \mathbb Q(\sqrt 2)\) over \(\mathbb Q\). The missing content is precisely ``\(k\) algebraically closed in \(B\)''. Adding the \emph{joint} hypotheses \texttt{[IsAlgClosed kbar]} \(+\) \texttt{[IsDomain B]} makes it true: \texttt{[IsDomain B]} kills the \(B = k \times k\) counterexample and \texttt{[IsAlgClosed kbar]} kills the \(\mathbb Q(\sqrt 2)/\mathbb Q\) counterexample. 2266	  By definition \texttt{Algebra.IsGeometricallyReduced}\,\(k\)\,\(F\) says \(F \otimes_k \bar k\) is reduced. Since \(F / k\) is finite, \(F \otimes_k \bar k\) is a finite-dimensional \(\bar k\)-algebra, hence Artinian, and decomposes uniquely as a finite product \(F \otimes_k \bar k \cong \prod_i F_i\) of Artinian local \(\bar k\)-algebras at its (finitely many) minimal primes. Reducedness of the product forces each Artinian local factor \(F_i\) to be a field, finite over \(\bar k\); algebraic closedness of \(\bar k\) then forces \(F_i = \bar k\).
2267	
2268	  Therefore \(F \otimes_k \bar k \cong \bar k^{r}\) for \(r = \#\{\text{minimal primes of } F \otimes_k \bar k\}\). The \(\bar k\)-dimension on both sides gives \([F : k] = \dim_{\bar k}(F \otimes_k \bar k) = r\). On the other hand, \(r\) equals the number of distinct \(k\)-algebra embeddings \(F \hookrightarrow \bar k\) (the closed points of \(\Spec(F \otimes_k \bar k)\)). The equality \([F : k] = \#\{\text{embeddings } F \hookrightarrow \bar k\}\) is precisely the criterion for separability of a finite field extension. Estimated build: \(\sim 30\)--\(50\) LOC.

98	[Albanese universal property of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) -- Milne III~\S 6, Proposition~6.1]
99	  \label{thm:albanese_universal_property}

104	
105	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Proposition~6.1, \S III.6, doc p.~104
106	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 110).
107	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
108	  % "PROPOSITION 6.1. Let \(P\) be a \(k\)-rational point on \(C\).  The map
109	  %  \(f^P\colon C\,\to\,J\) has the following universal property: for any
110	  %  map \(\varphi\colon C\to A\) from \(C\) into an abelian variety sending
111	  %  \(P\) to \(0\), there is a unique homomorphism \(\psi\colon J\to A\) such
112	  %  that \(\varphi=\psi\circ f^P\)."
113	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Proposition~6.1, \S III.6, p.~104.}
114	
115	  Let \(C\) be a complete nonsingular curve of genus \(g > 0\) over an algebraically
116	  closed field \(\bar k\), let \(P_0 \in C(\bar k)\) be a \(\bar k\)-rational point,
117	  and write \(J := \Pic^0_{C/k}\), \(\iota_{P_0} \colon C \to J\) for the
118	  Abel--Jacobi morphism of \cref{lem:abel_jacobi_morphism}. Then for every
119	  abelian variety \(A\) over \(\bar k\) and every pointed morphism
120	  \(\varphi \colon C \to A\) with \(\varphi(P_0) = \eta_A\), there exists a unique
121	  homomorphism of group schemes \(\psi \colon J \to A\) such that
122	  \(\varphi = \psi \circ \iota_{P_0}\).
123	\end{theorem}
